texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract:
texts.text: Abstract:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The scrubbing of hydrogen sulfide from air gas mixture is investigated in two porous polypropylene (PP) hollow fiber modules of different contact area and fiber packing fraction. The gas phase is circulated in the lumen of the fiber bore and the liquid phase in the shell in a non wetted mode, i.e. the membrane pores being filled with gas. A laminar parabolic velocity has been employed to describe the convective diffusive mass transport equation which has been solved analytically and numerically.
texts.text: The scrubbing of hydrogen sulfide from air gas mixture is investigated in two porous polypropylene (PP) hollow fiber modules of different contact area and fiber packing fraction. The gas phase is circulated in the lumen of the fiber bore and the liquid phase in the shell in a non wetted mode, i.e. the membrane pores being filled with gas. A laminar parabolic velocity has been employed to describe the convective diffusive mass transport equation which has been solved analytically and numerically.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The calculated extents of hydrogen sulfide depletion reasonably compares with the generated experimental results for both membrane modules. Up to 85 % of acid gas could be removed at gaseous flowrates of 200 cc/min for the large module and removals as high as 89 % at 10 cc/min have been observed for the smaller one. The overall mass transfer coefficients calculated from the experimental data, agree satisfactorily with those generated by the mathematical model. The relation of the dimensionless Sherwood number to the Graetz number is in a good agreement with the Levêque semi-analytical solution.
texts.text: The calculated extents of hydrogen sulfide depletion reasonably compares with the generated experimental results for both membrane modules. Up to 85 % of acid gas could be removed at gaseous flowrates of 200 cc/min for the large module and removals as high as 89 % at 10 cc/min have been observed for the smaller one. The overall mass transfer coefficients calculated from the experimental data, agree satisfactorily with those generated by the mathematical model. The relation of the dimensionless Sherwood number to the Graetz number is in a good agreement with the Levêque semi-analytical solution.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Introduction
texts.text: Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane based gas-liquid contactors has known in the last decade a considerable development. This technology has proven attractive applications in two broad areas, namely, the biological treatment of contaminated gas streams (trichloroethylene, propene, and other VOC`s), and the abiotic gas absorption of waste gases (sulfur dioxide, ammonia, etc…). The first shown application of this membrane based gas-liquid absorption process was the blood oxygenation using a hydrophobic flat PTFE where blood flows in one side and air or pure oxygen on the other side of the membrane (Esato and Eiseman 1975). Qi and Cussler (1985), Cooney and Jackson (1989) and Karoor and Sirkar (1993) have used microporous hollow fiber membranes devices to study the absorption of a number of gases (carbon dioxide, sulfur dioxide, ammonia, etc…) into various solvents or aqueous solutions (water, soda, MEA, etc…).
texts.text: The membrane based gas-liquid contactors has known in the last decade a considerable development. This technology has proven attractive applications in two broad areas, namely, the biological treatment of contaminated gas streams (trichloroethylene, propene, and other VOC`s), and the abiotic gas absorption of waste gases (sulfur dioxide, ammonia, etc…). The first shown application of this membrane based gas-liquid absorption process was the blood oxygenation using a hydrophobic flat PTFE where blood flows in one side and air or pure oxygen on the other side of the membrane (Esato and Eiseman 1975). Qi and Cussler (1985), Cooney and Jackson (1989) and Karoor and Sirkar (1993) have used microporous hollow fiber membranes devices to study the absorption of a number of gases (carbon dioxide, sulfur dioxide, ammonia, etc…) into various solvents or aqueous solutions (water, soda, MEA, etc…).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These devices resemble shell and tube heat exchangers as far as the configuration is concerned. The only difference is that in the hollow fiber membrane contactor, the fibres are randomly bundled and potted in the shell cartridge, whereas in heat exchangers, the tubes are arranged in a well defined geometrical pitch.
texts.text: These devices resemble shell and tube heat exchangers as far as the configuration is concerned. The only difference is that in the hollow fiber membrane contactor, the fibres are randomly bundled and potted in the shell cartridge, whereas in heat exchangers, the tubes are arranged in a well defined geometrical pitch.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Eventhough conventional absorption processes (bubble columns, packed and spray towers) have been used in the chemical industry for decades, the interdependence of the fluids to be contacted presents a real disadvantage that sometimes results in difficulties such as foaming, flooding and emulsions. The gas-liquid hollow fiber membrane contactor (HFMC) has proven to be a promising technique to overcome these shortcomings. Besides the non dispersive contact between fluids, this technique offers a larger, and easy to evaluate, interfacial contact area. Matson et al. (1983) reported that surface contact area developed by a fiber module could reach 8000 m
texts.text: Eventhough conventional absorption processes (bubble columns, packed and spray towers) have been used in the chemical industry for decades, the interdependence of the fluids to be contacted presents a real disadvantage that sometimes results in difficulties such as foaming, flooding and emulsions. The gas-liquid hollow fiber membrane contactor (HFMC) has proven to be a promising technique to overcome these shortcomings. Besides the non dispersive contact between fluids, this technique offers a larger, and easy to evaluate, interfacial contact area. Matson et al. texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, the presence of a membrane itself consists of another resistance to mass transfer not encountered in the absorption columns. This resistance could affect negatively the overall mass transfer and lower significantly the selectivity. This membrane resistance can be minimized by reducing the membrane thickness. Many authors, Qi and Cussler (1985) and Prasad and Sirkar (1988) have specifically investigated this membrane resistance.
texts.text: However, the presence of a membrane itself consists of another resistance to mass transfer not encountered in the absorption columns. This resistance could affect negatively the overall mass transfer and lower significantly the selectivity. This membrane resistance can be minimized by reducing the membrane thickness. Many authors, Qi and Cussler (1985) and Prasad and Sirkar (1988) have specifically investigated this membrane resistance.

texts.orig: These novel membrane based processes have been reviewed very concisely by Sirkar (1997), and thoroughly detailed by Gabelman and S. T. Hwang (1999).
texts.text: These novel membrane based processes have been reviewed very concisely by Sirkar (1997), and thoroughly detailed by Gabelman and S. T. Hwang (1999).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For gas-liquid contact devices, two types of membranes can be used: hydrophobic microporous polymer material such as polypropylene and dense polymeric material such as silicone rubber (polydimethylsiloxane). The microporous material presents a higher permeability whereas dense material could be advantageous in cases where a specific selectivity is desired.
texts.text: For gas-liquid contact devices, two types of membranes can be used: hydrophobic microporous polymer material such as polypropylene and dense polymeric material such as silicone rubber (polydimethylsiloxane). The microporous material presents a higher permeability whereas dense material could be advantageous in cases where a specific selectivity is desired.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper investigates the absorption of hydrogen sulfide into water through a hydrophobic microporous hollow fiber membrane module operated in a non-wetted mode with the gas flowing in the fiber side and the liquid in the shell. The observed experimental results are compared with a numerical simulation. Two cartridges have been used in this study, a close packed and a wide spaced fiber bundle potting, to visualize the effect of the contact surface area on the mass transfer rates.
texts.text: This paper investigates the absorption of hydrogen sulfide into water through a hydrophobic microporous hollow fiber membrane module operated in a non-wetted mode with the gas flowing in the fiber side and the liquid in the shell. The observed experimental results are compared with a numerical simulation. Two cartridges have been used in this study, a close packed and a wide spaced fiber bundle potting, to visualize the effect of the contact surface area on the mass transfer rates.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Theoretical background
texts.text: Theoretical background

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A membrane contactor is a process that achieves gas-liquid (or liquid-liquid) mass transfer by passing the fluid to be scrubbed on the opposite side of the absorbing fluid of a microporous membrane.
texts.text: A membrane contactor is a process that achieves gas-liquid (or liquid-liquid) mass transfer by passing the fluid to be scrubbed on the opposite side of the absorbing fluid of a microporous membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The contaminated gas may flow in the tube or shell side, in concurrent or countercurrent flow with the solvent stream. The membranes could be hydrophilic or hydrophobic, that is pores could be gas-filled (dry) or liquid-filled (wet).
texts.text: The contaminated gas may flow in the tube or shell side, in concurrent or countercurrent flow with the solvent stream. The membranes could be hydrophilic or hydrophobic, that is pores could be gas-filled (dry) or liquid-filled (wet).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The gas-liquid interface is at the pore mouth of the membrane since the liquid and gas phases are flowing on each side of the membrane without any phase dispersion on both side, and this is achieved by a careful control of the pressure difference across the membrane barrier.
texts.text: The gas-liquid interface is at the pore mouth of the membrane since the liquid and gas phases are flowing on each side of the membrane without any phase dispersion on both side, and this is achieved by a careful control of the pressure difference across the membrane barrier.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two operating modes depending on the gas and absorbent pressure on either tube or shell side of the membrane based gas liquid contactor can be considered, namely the wetted and non wetted (dry) modes.
texts.text: Two operating modes depending on the gas and absorbent pressure on either tube or shell side of the membrane based gas liquid contactor can be considered, namely the wetted and non wetted (dry) modes.

The gas liquid interface is at the pore mouth of the hydrophilic membrane on the gas side.

The absorbent does not cross the membrane pore mouth unless the liquid pressure exceeds a certain critical value. The gas liquid interface is at the pore mouth of the hydrophobic membrane on the liquid side.
texts.text: has to be lower than that of the liquid in order to prevent any dispersion of gases in the liquid in form of bubbles. The absorbent does not cross the membrane pore mouth unless the liquid pressure exceeds a certain critical value. The gas liquid interface is at the pore mouth of the hydrophobic membrane on the liquid side.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The preference of one operation mode on the other is dictated by the importance of the chemical reaction accompanying the absorption. When the absorption is simply physical, the non-wetted mode is suitable to take advantage of the higher diffusivity in the gas phase, hence the mass transfer is controlled by the liquid phase. However, when a fast or instantaneous chemical reaction is taking place in the liquid phase, the wetted mode is indicated, and consequently a gas phase mass transfer control.
texts.text: The preference of one operation mode on the other is dictated by the importance of the chemical reaction accompanying the absorption. When the absorption is simply physical, the non-wetted mode is suitable to take advantage of the higher diffusivity in the gas phase, hence the mass transfer is controlled by the liquid phase. However, when a fast or instantaneous chemical reaction is taking place in the liquid phase, the wetted mode is indicated, and consequently a gas phase mass transfer control.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hence, as depicted in figures 1 and 2, we have three mass transfer resistances in series: the gas boundary layer film, the membrane stagnant layer, and the liquid boundary layer film resistances. Since, in the non-wetted mode, the membrane pores are gas filled, the gas phase mass transfer resistance is likely to be more important than that in the liquid phase, except in the case where a chemical reaction is accompanying the physical absorption or when the gas solubility is not high.
texts.text: Hence, as depicted in figures 1 and 2, we have three mass transfer resistances in series: the gas boundary layer film, the membrane stagnant layer, and the liquid boundary layer film resistances. Since, in the non-wetted mode, the membrane pores are gas filled, the gas phase mass transfer resistance is likely to be more important than that in the liquid phase, except in the case where a chemical reaction is accompanying the physical absorption or when the gas solubility is not high.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Unlike the classical membrane applications (reverse osmosis, ultrafiltration, microfiltration, gas permeation etc.), the separation driving force is the concentration gradient rather than a pressure gradient.
texts.text: Unlike the classical membrane applications (reverse osmosis, ultrafiltration, microfiltration, gas permeation etc.), the separation driving force is the concentration gradient rather than a pressure gradient.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (2001). Module I has a higher number of fibers, then a larger contact surface area. The membrane specifications that were not supplied by the manufacturer have been estimated. The inner contact area stands for the total surface area based on the inner fiber diameter. The packing fraction is the ratio of the total outer diameter fiber volume to the shell volume. The surface to volume fraction is the ratio of the active surface area to the shell volume.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The built experimental setup is shown in Fig.3. The gas and liquid phase, separated by the membrane wall, are circulated on a countercurrent flow pattern. The laboratory setup was run (for both contactor modules) at different gas flow rates, circulating the gas continuously inside the fiber bundle and the liquid batchwise in the shell of the contactor. The hydrogen sulfide, a reagent grade supplied by BOC, received with a 1000 ppm v/v concentration, is diluted into air and the concentration broken down to 100 ppm v/v, which is equivalent to  moles/cc at a 15 psig inlet gas pressure. The gas flow rate is measured by a gas flowmeter, and the inlet and outlet gas pressures are controlled by pressure gages (0-30 psig).
texts.text: The built experimental setup is shown in Fig.3. The gas and liquid phase, separated by the membrane wall, are circulated on a countercurrent flow pattern. The laboratory setup was run (for both contactor modules) at different gas flow rates, circulating the gas continuously inside the fiber bundle and the liquid batchwise in the shell of the contactor. The hydrogen sulfide, a reagent grade supplied by BOC, received with a 1000 ppm v/v concentration, is diluted into air and the concentration broken down to 100 ppm v/v, which is equivalent to  moles/cc at a 15 psig inlet gas pressure. The gas flow rate is measured by a gas flowmeter, and the inlet and outlet gas pressures are controlled by pressure gages (0-30 psig).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since we operate the absorption module on a non-wetted mode, the liquid pressure was carefully maintained at 15 psig, and the gas phase pressure at 20 psig.
texts.text: Since we operate the absorption module on a non-wetted mode, the liquid pressure was carefully maintained at 15 psig, and the gas phase pressure at 20 psig.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The inlet and outlet gas mixture concentrations were controlled by means of a Custodian gas detector, supplied by Crocown Detection Instruments Ltd (), after which the outlet gas is vented through the exhaust hood. The accuracy of this probe is 1 ppm v/v in a range of 50 ppm.
texts.text: The inlet and outlet gas mixture concentrations were controlled by means of a Custodian gas detector, supplied by Crocown Detection Instruments Ltd (), after which the outlet gas is vented through the exhaust hood. The accuracy of this probe is 1 ppm v/v in a range of 50 ppm.

texts.orig: Numerical Solution Procedure
texts.text: Numerical Solution Procedure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Solving analytically this problem is a quite tedious task. However, many numerical techniques can be used to solve this non-linear partial differential equation, such as the finite difference discretization, quasi-linearization techniques, and the orthogonal collocation methods.
texts.text: Solving analytically this problem is a quite tedious task. However, many numerical techniques can be used to solve this non-linear partial differential equation, such as the finite difference discretization, quasi-linearization techniques, and the orthogonal collocation methods.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The orthogonal collocation, introduced by Villadsen and Stewart (1967), is a technique categorized as a method of weight residuals and has been shown by Finlayson (1971) to be superior in some respects to the finite difference approaches and yield more accurate results with less computational effort compared to the conventional methods.
texts.text: The orthogonal collocation, introduced by Villadsen and Stewart (1967), is a technique categorized as a method of weight residuals and has been shown by Finlayson (1971) to be superior in some respects to the finite difference approaches and yield more accurate results with less computational effort compared to the conventional methods.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Extensive and successful applications of this method to dynamic simulations of chemical processes have been made (Stewart et al. 1984, Kao and Yan 1987, Michelsen 1994, and Tessendorf et. al. 1999).
texts.text: Extensive and successful applications of this method to dynamic simulations of chemical processes have been made (Stewart et al. 1984, Kao and Yan 1987, Michelsen 1994, and Tessendorf et. al. 1999).

texts.orig: Figures 7 and 8 illustrate the  extent of concentration depletion in the gas outlet stream as a function of gas flowrate. Experimental data and numerical simulation are presented for both HFM modules I and II.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These figures show that a maximum acid gas removal of 85 % has been achieved at a gas flowrate of 200 cc/mn with module I and 89 % at 10 cc/mn for module II. The contact surface area, which could be increased by using a larger number of fibers or by increasing the fiber bundle length in the module, permits an efficient diffusional mass-transfer. Consequently, because module I has a larger contact surface area, a higher amount of  is present in the outlet gas stream in module II compared to module I for the same gas flowrate. The model solution agrees with the experimental data with a maximum deviation of up to 12 % due mainly to the estimation of the porosity to tortuosity ratio  involved in the determination of the membrane resistance . The essential reason to this difference is probably that the membrane assumed to be completely dry was partially wetted as a result of the gas and liquid fluctuating pressures.
texts.text: These figures show that a maximum acid gas removal of 85 % has been achieved at a gas flowrate of 200 cc/mn with module I and 89 % at 10 cc/mn for module II. The contact surface area, which could be increased by using a larger number of fibers or by increasing the fiber bundle length in the module, permits an efficient diffusional mass-transfer. Consequently, because module I has a larger contact surface area, a higher amount of  is present in the outlet gas stream in module II compared to module I for the same gas flowrate. The model solution agrees with the experimental data with a maximum deviation of up to 12 % due mainly to the estimation of the porosity to tortuosity ratio  involved in the determination of the membrane resistance . The essential reason to this difference is probably that the membrane assumed to be completely dry was partially wetted as a result of the gas and liquid fluctuating pressures.

texts.orig: The numerically calculated results are in reasonable agreement with the experimentally generated data, specially for module I, module II having a narrow cartridge has shown channeling problems during its operation. The deviation of the laboratory collected data from the simulated results is mainly due to experimental errors and the uncertainty of the hypothetical assumptions involved in the theoretical model development.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Conclusion
texts.text: Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The experimental studies conducted with the polypropylene hollow fiber membrane contactors have demonstrated that this technology can effectively remove with substantial efficiencies of up to 89 % for inlet concentrations of  100 ppm v. This study has also shown that the gas-liquid absorption of  in a hollow fiber contactor is mostly membrane resistance controlled. The overall mass transfer coefficient of the absorption process are in the same order of magnitude for the two modules and were found to vary between  and . The overall mass transfer coefficient was found to vary as a linear function of the gas velocity with an almost negligible slope, but was insensitive to the inlet gas concentration.
texts.text: The experimental studies conducted with the polypropylene hollow fiber membrane contactors have demonstrated that this technology can effectively remove with substantial efficiencies of up to 89 % for inlet concentrations of  100 ppm v. This study has also shown that the gas-liquid absorption of  in a hollow fiber contactor is mostly membrane resistance controlled. The overall mass transfer coefficient of the absorption process are in the same order of magnitude for the two modules and were found to vary between  and . The overall mass transfer coefficient was found to vary as a linear function of the gas velocity with an almost negligible slope, but was insensitive to the inlet gas concentration.
